preamble: The transformation of the high front rounded vowel [y] (or the palatal glide [j] in similar phonetic environments) into the 'ain' segment (representing [eɪn] or similar nasalized diphthongs) is documented in specific English dialects and informal speech reductions.

examples.note: Word-specific shift occurring by analogy with 'ain't', where the front vowel shifts toward a nasalized diphthong.
Morpho-phonetic shift where the palatal glide [j] ('y') of the pronoun is followed by or merges into the 'ain' segment of the negation.

sources: Phonological research on 'ain't' analogy and auxiliary reduction.
